segments.title: The Hook
segments.transcriptSegment: Every developer mass-installs VS Code extensions without reading what they actually do. Some of those extensions have full access to your file system, your terminal, and your clipboard. You're giving a stranger the keys to your entire machine.
segments.aiVideoPrompt: Close-up of a developer installing VS Code extensions carelessly, clicking Install buttons rapidly. Camera slowly zooms into the screen showing extension permissions. Dark, moody lighting with blue monitor glow reflecting off their face. The extensions panel fills with suspicious icons. Cinematic, unsettling atmosphere.

segments.title: Under The Hood
segments.transcriptSegment: Let's break down how VS Code extensions work under the hood. When you install an extension, it runs inside a Node.js process that VS Code spawns for it. This process has almost no sandboxing. It can read any file on your disk, execute shell commands, and make network requests to any server. There's no permission popup, no confirmation dialog. You click install, and it has full access.

segments.title: Code Exfiltration
segments.transcriptSegment: Here's the scary part. A malicious extension can do three things without you ever noticing. First, it can exfiltrate your code. Every file you open, every keystroke you type, can be silently sent to a remote server. Your proprietary code, your API keys, your environment variables, all of it.

segments.title: Code Injection
segments.transcriptSegment: Second, it can inject code into your projects. Imagine an extension that quietly modifies your package.json to add a dependency that looks legitimate but contains a backdoor. You'd never notice because the diff looks like a normal dependency update.

segments.title: Persistence Beyond Uninstall
segments.transcriptSegment: Third, it can persist beyond uninstall. Some extensions write to your shell profile, your git hooks, or your system startup scripts. Even after you remove the extension, the payload stays behind.

segments.title: Marketplace Reality
segments.transcriptSegment: The VS Code marketplace has over forty thousand extensions. Microsoft reviews the most popular ones, but the long tail is essentially unmoderated. Last year, researchers found eighteen extensions with over seven million combined installs that were secretly mining cryptocurrency.

segments.title: Three Safety Checks
segments.transcriptSegment: So what should you do? Check three things before installing any extension. One, look at the publisher. Verified publishers have a blue checkmark. Two, check the source code. Every extension links to its GitHub repo. If there's no repo, that's a red flag. Three, review the permissions in the package.json manifest. If a color theme extension is requesting terminal access, something is very wrong.

segments.title: The Closing
segments.transcriptSegment: The tools you use to write code shouldn't be the ones that compromise it. A five second check before clicking install can save you from a very bad day.
segments.aiVideoPrompt: Wide shot of a developer pausing before clicking Install on a VS Code extension. They examine the extension details carefully — checking publisher, source code link, permissions. Calm, deliberate. The screen shows a suspicious extension with no repository. They close the tab and move on. Clean, bright lighting. Empowering tone.
